posts.title: Building a Developer Portfolio OS
posts.excerpt: How I architected this site to work like a living system—live GitHub stats, a blog engine, CI/CD automation, and a real contact pipeline—all as a zero-cost static site on GitHub Pages.
posts.content:
Most developer portfolios are just a pretty HTML file with your name on it. I wanted something that behaves more like a living system—one that reflects actual work in real time, accepts contact without a broken mailto link, and validates itself before deploying.

The Stack Decision

The constraint was cost: $0/month, no server to babysit. That pointed straight at GitHub Pages for hosting with GitHub Actions for CI. The entire stack is static, but with selective dynamic behavior layered on through client-side JavaScript.

The CMS: Just JSON

For a solo portfolio, a full CMS like Contentful or Sanity is engineering for its own sake. Instead, blog posts live in assets/data/posts.json—plain JSON with HTML content fields. The "CMS" workflow is: write a post in your editor, commit, push. GitHub Actions validates the HTML, GitHub Pages deploys within seconds.

The blog page reads the JSON on load and routes by URL parameter: blog.html?post=slug. No build step, no server, no redirects to configure.

Live GitHub Stats

The GitHub REST API doesn't require authentication for public data. On page load, initGitHubStats() fetches all public repos, aggregates total stars and forks, then fires parallel languages_url requests for the top 10 repos to compute a language distribution. The numbers animate into view when the section scrolls into the viewport using IntersectionObserver.

CI/CD Pipeline

The GitHub Actions workflow runs on every push and pull request to main. It does two things: validates all HTML files with htmlhint (catching unclosed tags, duplicate IDs, missing titles) and audits local asset references by parsing src and href attributes to confirm every referenced local file exists. Broken asset links fail the build before they reach production.

Contact Form

The contact section uses Formspree as a backend proxy. The form submits via fetch() so the page never reloads—success and error states appear inline. Formspree handles spam filtering, stores submissions, and forwards them to email. Free tier covers 50 submissions per month, which is plenty for a portfolio.

What I Learned

The biggest insight was that a static site can feel just as dynamic as a server-rendered one for the use cases a portfolio actually needs. The GitHub API replaces a database for project data. JSON replaces a CMS for blog content. A form service replaces a backend for contact. The result is zero hosting cost, zero maintenance overhead, and automatic deploys on every git push.

posts.title: IT Support in Academia: What 30+ Classrooms Taught Me About Reliability
posts.excerpt: Two years of keeping classroom AV and network systems running—before every lecture, across 30+ rooms—reshaped how I think about reliability, documentation, and fixing problems before they happen.
posts.content:
The moment a professor walks into a room and the projector doesn't work is a small disaster. Fifteen minutes before a lecture, with 40 students filing in, there is no time for a methodical root-cause analysis. You need to know the system well enough to fix it in two minutes or route around it in one.

Working as an IT Support Specialist for UNCG's Information Technology Services put me in that position hundreds of times across 30+ classrooms. Here's what it actually taught me.

Documentation Is the Product

Every resolved ticket in ServiceNow is a knowledge article waiting to be written. Early on I'd close a ticket and move on. Later I started documenting the fix—what symptom, what diagnostic step revealed the cause, what fixed it. Three months later when the same Extron AV system in a different room showed the same symptom, I had a runbook. Resolution time dropped from 20 minutes to 4.

The insight: documentation isn't overhead on top of support work. It is the support work, just with a longer time horizon.

The Difference Between Reactive and Preventive

Reactive support means you learn about a problem when a professor emails you at 8:45 AM. Preventive support means you find the failing HDMI cable on Tuesday afternoon during a walkthrough, before anyone notices.

I started doing a quick pre-semester sweep of every room on my coverage list. Checking cable connections, firmware versions, network connectivity, display calibration. The rate of mid-class failures dropped noticeably. The work shifted from firefighting to gardening—routine tending that keeps things alive.

ITIL in Practice Isn't What You Read in a Textbook

ITIL talks about incident management, problem management, and change management as distinct processes. In practice at a university IT department, they blur constantly. A projector failure is an incident. When the same projector fails four times in a semester, it becomes a problem record. The fix—replacing the unit—goes through change management to schedule downtime.

Understanding which mode you're in changes what you do next. Incident: restore service fast. Problem: find the root cause, not just the workaround. Change: coordinate with stakeholders so the fix doesn't break something else.

What This Built

Two years of classroom support built habits I carry into every technical project: write down what you find, look for patterns across incidents, prefer preventive work over reactive, and communicate status before anyone has to ask. Those habits matter as much in software engineering as in IT support—maybe more.

posts.title: From ERD to API: Designing the Dress Web Store Database
posts.excerpt: A walkthrough of designing the Dress Web Store from the entity-relationship diagram forward—how the schema decisions shaped the API, and what I'd do differently now.
posts.content:
The Dress Web Store project started with a blank page in ERDPlus and the question: what data does an e-commerce platform actually need to store, and how should it be structured?

That question turned out to be more interesting than I expected.

Starting with the ERD

The core entities were obvious: Product, Customer, Order, OrderItem. The relationships between them were where the design decisions lived.

The first version had Order containing a direct list of products. Simple, but it violates first normal form—you can't store a repeating group in a relational column. The fix is the junction table: OrderItem with foreign keys to both Order and Product, plus a quantity and a unit_price field.

Why store unit_price on OrderItem instead of joining to Product.price? Because prices change. If a customer ordered a dress last month at $89 and you change the price to $109 today, the historical order record must reflect what they actually paid. Denormalizing the price onto the order line item is intentional.

PostgreSQL on Neon

I used PostgreSQL hosted on Neon for the database. Neon's serverless Postgres gives you a connection string, automatic scaling, and a free tier that's more than enough for a course project. The DBeaver GUI made it easy to inspect the schema visually, write ad-hoc queries during development, and verify that inserts were landing correctly.

One thing I'd do differently: set up proper database migrations from the start instead of running ALTER TABLE statements manually. Even for a small project, a migration file per schema change makes the history clear and the deployment reproducible.

CRUD API Design

The REST API followed standard resource-per-entity conventions. Each entity maps to a base URL path, and HTTP verbs carry the operation semantics:

  • GET /products — list with optional filter/sort query params
  • POST /orders — create order with line items in the body
  • PUT /orders/:id — update order status
  • DELETE /products/:id — remove from catalog

The interesting one was POST /orders. Creating an order isn't just an insert—it's an insert into Order, followed by multiple inserts into OrderItem, each with the current product price snapshotted. That's a transaction. If any insert fails, the whole order rolls back. Getting transaction semantics right in the API layer (not just the database layer) was the most important backend lesson from the project.

The Figma Side

Designing the UI mockups in Figma before writing a line of frontend code was more valuable than I expected. It forced decisions about information hierarchy: what does the product listing card show versus the product detail page? How does the checkout flow handle out-of-stock items? Resolving those questions in a static mockup costs an hour. Resolving them mid-implementation costs a refactor.

What I'd Change

Looking back: I'd add a Category entity from the start (we added it late and it required schema changes), write proper migration files, and add basic input validation at the API layer—not just in the database constraints. The project demonstrated end-to-end SDLC skills, but it also made clear that the design phase is where most of the important decisions actually happen.
